metadata.source: Complete hand sweep patch for mushroom safety wording, poisonous look-alikes, and full starter bolete wording pass
notes: Adds missing poisonous look-alike entries that edible mushrooms refer to.
Strengthens caution wording for beginner-risk mushrooms.
Provides a full by-hand wording sweep across the starter bolete pack.

mushroom_profile.summary: Deadly brown wood-growing mushroom containing amatoxins. Grows on decaying hardwood and conifer wood.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Treat as a major danger species, not a food mushroom.

records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Even small amounts can cause delayed but life-threatening poisoning.
records.medicinal_uses: None for food or amateur medicinal use.
records.effects_on_body: Contains amatoxins that can cause delayed vomiting and diarrhea followed by severe liver injury and possible death.
records.comparison_notes: Velvet foot has a white spore print and lacks a persistent ring. Galerina shows rusty brown spores, yellow-brown to rusty gills, and usually a ring or ring zone.
records.habitat_detail: Look for this mushroom on dead wood, logs, and stumps in both hardwood and conifer settings. Small brown wood mushrooms should not be guessed at.

mushroom_profile.summary: Poisonous lawn and open-ground parasol often confused with edible parasol types.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Treat as a major GI-risk look-alike.

records.effects_on_body: Can cause intense gastrointestinal distress and is a classic dangerous parasol look-alike.
records.comparison_notes: Parasol mushrooms do not produce the green spore print that gives this mushroom its name.
records.habitat_detail: Look for this mushroom in lawns, grassy edges, and disturbed open ground. Large parasol mushrooms from open ground need a spore-print check if there is any doubt.

records.culinary_uses: Technically edible, but not recommended for foraging because it can be confused with deadly Galerina. This is not a good beginner mushroom.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid treating this as a casual edible. The real risk is deadly confusion with Galerina and other small brown wood mushrooms.
records.comparison_notes: Deadly galerina usually shows a ring or ring zone and rusty-brown spores and gills. Velvet foot lacks a persistent ring and has a white spore print.

records.edibility_detail: Not a beginner mushroom. Some people get significant GI upset even from correctly identified material, and the genus also overlaps dangerous wood-growing look-alikes.

records.comparison_notes: Jack-o'-lantern grows from wood, has true gills, and is poisonous. Smooth chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt ridges instead of knife-like true gills.
Jack-o'-lantern grows from wood and has sharp true gills. True chanterelles grow from the ground and have blunt forked ridges rather than blade-like gills.
Green-spored parasol is a poisonous look-alike. Large lawn parasols should not be eaten without a correct spore print and full structural match.
Green-spored parasol is poisonous and can look deceptively similar. Do not trust big parasol-type mushrooms from open ground without a full ID.

records.edibility_detail: Only for experienced collectors. White-gilled or young button-stage mushrooms can be confused with deadly white Amanitas, so this is not a beginner edible.
records.comparison_notes: Destroying angel keeps white gills and has a volva at the base. Field mushrooms should develop pink to chocolate-brown gills and must be checked from top to base.

records.edibility_detail: Only for experienced collectors. The danger here is confusion with deadly white Amanitas while still young or poorly examined.
records.comparison_notes: Destroying angel has white gills and a sac-like base. Meadow mushrooms should show pink to brown maturing gills and must be checked carefully from cap to base.

records.edibility_detail: A solid edible, but still part of the red-and-staining bolete crowd. Use the full mushroom and rule out harsher red-pored boletes rather than trusting cap color alone.
records.comparison_notes: Check pore color, how strongly the flesh blues when bruised, and whether the stalk is smooth or netted. Red-and-yellow boletes should be treated as a group that rewards careful comparison, not casual guessing.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended for beginners. Red-pored blue-staining boletes are easy to mishandle if you do not already know the group well.
records.edibility_detail: Treat this as an advanced-ID bolete, not a casual edible. The issue is not that it is automatically poisonous, but that red-pored boletes are a confusion-prone group.
records.comparison_notes: Use the whole mushroom, not the cap color alone. Red pores, strong blue staining, heavy netting on the stalk, and the broader dangerous red-pored bolete group all matter here.

records.edibility_detail: A worthwhile bolete, but still one to confirm fully. Brown boletes should be checked for bitter taste, stalk netting, staining, and host association before they are trusted.
records.comparison_notes: Check for the long stalk, yellow pores, netting, and a mild rather than bitter taste. Do not trust a brown cap alone.

records.culinary_uses: Edible Suillus with soft texture; many foragers consider it acceptable rather than exceptional.
records.edibility_detail: Usually treated as edible, but Suillus species can be a mixed bag in texture and personal tolerance. The real work here is confirming the pine association and the glandular-dotted stalk.
records.comparison_notes: Check the pine host, sticky yellow cap, yellow pores, and dotted stalk. Separate ringed from ringless Suillus and do not assume every sticky yellow bolete is the same species.

records.edibility_detail: Edible, but mainly for people already comfortable with Suillus. Host tree, ring absence, pore droplets when young, and glandular dots on the stalk matter more than people think.
records.comparison_notes: Look for a pine associate with a sticky cap, glandular dots, and no true ring. Compare carefully against the other ringless Suillus species in the same habitat.

records.culinary_uses: Edible, but mostly of interest to people already sorting white-pine Suillus species.
records.edibility_detail: This is not a beginner bolete. Pale sticky Suillus species need to be tied to host tree and stalk details rather than treated as interchangeable.
records.comparison_notes: Use the white-pine association, pale cap and pore surface, and glandular dots on the stalk. A pale sticky cap alone is not enough.

records.culinary_uses: Edible, though more notable visually than gastronomically.
records.edibility_detail: A pine-associated Suillus that is easier to recognize than some of its cousins, but still best confirmed by patterning, host tree, and pore surface rather than cap color alone.
records.comparison_notes: The dry, patterned reddish cap helps, but confirm the pore surface and pine association before trusting it.

records.edibility_detail: Usually edible, but still part of a slippery, pine-loving group that needs host-tree and stalk-detail work. Not the best bolete for lazy identification.
records.comparison_notes: Short stalk, sticky cap, yellow pores, and pine association help. Compare carefully against other ringless Suillus species with glandular dots or soft yellow pore surfaces.

records.culinary_uses: Edible Suillus tied to tamarack / larch.
records.edibility_detail: A decent edible if correctly identified, but the host tree is part of the identification. A ringed sticky yellow Suillus should still be checked carefully instead of assumed.
records.comparison_notes: The tamarack / larch association and the ring matter. Do not collapse every sticky yellow ringed Suillus into one bucket.

records.culinary_uses: Edible Suillus, but not one to trust casually.
records.edibility_detail: This is a ringless pine-associated Suillus that needs host-tree and stalk-detail confirmation. It stays in the advanced-interest category until image coverage improves.
records.comparison_notes: Use the dotted stalk, pore surface, sticky cap, and pine association. Right now this entry especially needs full-structure comparison rather than faith.

records.edibility_detail: A yellowish hardwood bolete that still needs a full brown-bolete check: host tree, staining, pore color, and taste all matter.
records.comparison_notes: Use the ash association, yellow pores, and overall mild brown-bolete profile. A plain brown cap never proves anything by itself.

records.edibility_detail: A respected edible, but still one that should be checked for blue bruising, smooth stalk, and mild taste. Brown boletes only look simple from across the room.
records.comparison_notes: Look for the bay-brown cap, yellow to olive pores, blue bruising, and smooth stalk. Separate it from bitter Tylopilus-type boletes and from netted-stalk brown boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Not ideal for beginners. Leccinum species are edible for many people, but host tree, bruising changes, and species-level differences matter more than people think.
records.comparison_notes: The rough dotted stalk is only the start. Use host tree, cap color, pore color, and flesh-color changes before trusting a Leccinum.

records.edibility_detail: An orange-capped scaber-stalk bolete, but still not one to treat casually. Host tree and flesh-color changes matter, and the whole Leccinum group deserves patience.
records.comparison_notes: Use the birch association, orange cap, and scabered stalk together. Orange cap plus rough stalk is not enough by itself.

records.edibility_detail: A real edible, but not a beginner mushroom. Aspen-associated orange-capped Leccinum species need host-tree and bruising checks, not just a glance at the cap.
records.comparison_notes: Use the aspen association, orange cap, and rough stalk, then compare flesh-color changes and the exact host tree.

records.edibility_detail: Another orange-capped scaber-stalk bolete that needs host-tree confidence and whole-mushroom comparison. This is not a plug-and-play edible.
records.comparison_notes: Use the oak or hardwood association, orange cap, and scabers, then separate it from other orange-capped Leccinum by the full structure and host.

records.edibility_detail: This stays in the advanced-interest category until image coverage improves. It belongs to a look-alike-heavy scaber-stalk group where host tree and bruising patterns matter.
records.comparison_notes: Treat this as a species that needs full confirmation, especially because the current visual coverage is thin. Compare host tree, stalk scabers, cap tone, and flesh-color changes.

records.culinary_uses: Edible, but usually considered mediocre rather than a prize species.
records.edibility_detail: Worth learning more as an identification exercise than as a top food target. The cracked cap and mixed staining traits can be confusing within the broader xerocomoid boletes.
records.comparison_notes: Use the cracked cap showing reddish flesh, softer yellow to olive pores, and the overall smaller xerocomoid build. Do not overrate it as a food mushroom.

records.edibility_detail: This is one of the Tylopilus-type boletes where pore color and taste matter. Brown cap alone is worthless; you need the whole set of traits.
records.comparison_notes: Check for pinkish pores, netting on the stalk, and whether the taste stays mild instead of distinctly bitter. Brown boletes split fast once you start checking those details.

records.culinary_uses: Edible when young, but mainly memorable as an oddball bolete rather than a major food prize.
records.edibility_detail: Easy-looking only because it is weird. Age matters, texture matters, and this one is best treated as a distinctive specialty bolete rather than a default edible.
records.comparison_notes: The shaggy dark cap and stalk, gray-black pore aging, and odd heavy build make it stand out. Confirm the overall dark scaly look rather than a single feature.

records.culinary_uses: Several are edible, but this is not a beginner group.
records.edibility_detail: This group has edible members, but it is not ideal for beginners because host tree, bruising changes, and species-level differences matter more than people think.
records.comparison_notes: Rough dotted stalks alone are not enough. Use host tree, cap color, flesh color changes, and the whole structure before trusting a scaber-stalk bolete ID.
